The Simplified Kraus-Turner Mixed Layer Model for Hybrid Coordinates 
(KTB) 
 
The simplified Kraus-Turner slab mixed layer model (KTB) does not carry the mixed layer 
thickness as a full prognostic variable, in contrast to model KTA. Bleck (2002) used the KTB 
model in his initial global simulation using HYCOM. The tradeoff between using KTA and KTB 
is between improved computational efficiency and reduced accuracy. Since the KTA model is far 
from perfect, this simplified model is adequate for many purposes. 
Kraus-Turner mixed layer models compute the change of mixed layer thickness over a small 
time interval t
Δ . They require the old mixed layer information, which can be viewed as the 
cumulative result of past applications of the Kraus-Turner algorithm, as the initial condition. The 
KTB model contains a simple diagnostic procedure to find the new depth of the mixed layer base 
to avoid the necessity of unmixing, and to avoid the ambiguities of constructing a representative 
mixed layer velocity field to advect the mixed layer base (which as a material variable needs to 
follow the flow). 
Beginning at the top of the water column (layer 1), the algorithm searches for the first layer k  
whose density exceeds the average density 
r of the overlying layers combined (layers 1 through 
1k − ). This layer is assumed to contain the mixed layer base. Layer k  is divided into sublayers 
with different densities. The density upr  of the upper sublayer is set equal to r while the 
density lor  of the lower sublayer is assigned a value within the range ( )1kkrr ++ , for example 
( )1 /2lokkrrr +=+ . Density conservation during sublayer formation yields the depth of the 
interface separating the sublayers, which is taken to be the mixed layer depth. This pressure 
interface is given by

Assignment of temperature/salinity values for the depth interval ( )10, kp +  follows the above

Given the nonlinear nature of the equation of state, the upper and lower sublayer densities 
must be recomputed after temperature and salinity have been homogenized over layers 1 through 
1k −  and unmixed in layer k . After this, the density profile, characterized by homogeneous 
conditions between the surface and pressure mp  and a density discontinuity at that pressure, is 
provided to the K-T TKE algorithm to calculate the new mixed layer depth. Temperature and 
salinity are homogenized over the new mixed layer depth, and the surface fluxes are distributed 
over the same depth range. The resulting temperature and salinity profiles are then projected 
back onto the original hybrid coordinate profile. The sublayer information is not saved. 
It is important to note that the sublayer formation and deletion cycle by itself does not cause a 
drift in the /TS  profile provided that layers 1 through 1k −  are fully homogenized to begin 
with. In other words, the original /TS  profile is recovered if 0tΔ→ . Initial experiments with 
this scheme indicate that best results are obtained by assigning lor a value closer to kr  than to 
1kr + . To skip the necessity of forming sublayers, set lokrr=  (i.e., 0q = ). The /TS  profile 
transmitted to the K-T TKE algorithm in this case is obtained from the original profile by 
homogenizing temperature and salinity down to depth kp  and setting mkpp = . 
 
REFERENCE 
 
Bleck, R., 2002: An oceanic general circulation model framed in hybrid isopycnic-Cartesian 
coordinates. Ocean Modelling, 4, 55-88.

catalog_entry.summary: This document describes the Simplified Kraus-Turner Mixed Layer Model for Hybrid Coordinates (KTB) used in HYCOM, focusing on its computational efficiency and method of determining mixed layer depth without unmixing.
